contest.title: 2024 ICPC Asia Taichung Regional Contest (Unrated, Online Mirror, ICPC Rules, Preferably Teams)

sources.notes: Read the complete statement, all five samples, and the constraints n <= 5e5, heights <= 1e9, and pairwise-distinct b_i.

Read Problem J in the official Taichung analysis. It gives the fixed-split adjusted capacities, Hall-style rank condition, monotonic-stack sweep, suffix range-add counters, and count-of-minus-one formula.
Actually read the solution page, including the complete Cartesian-tree recurrence and code plus independent interval-expansion and fixed-split explanations.
Read the CF2041J section. It independently models a mountain by inserting bottles from largest to smallest at interval endpoints and derives an O(n log n) blocked-interval sweep.

Independently proved the sorted-assignment/Hall criterion, minus-one slack count, and Cartesian-tree recurrence. Brute force, the fixed-split formula, and the Cartesian recurrence agreed on 2,100 exhaustive and 1,330 random small instances.

annotation.summary: Each distinct front bottle may keep height b_i or become b_i-1, then all bottles must be permuted into a nondecreasing-then-nonincreasing mountain under position ceilings a_i. Find the minimum number of decrements, or report that even decrementing every bottle cannot work.
annotation.constraints: 1 <= n <= 5e5; 1 <= a_i,b_i <= 1e9; all b_i are distinct; 5 seconds and 1024 MB. The intended algorithms need O(n log n) time and O(n) memory.
annotation.core_idea: Sort b. Subtracting at most one never reverses two distinct integer heights, so a mountain can be formed by inserting bottles from largest to smallest at the ends of a growing interval. Build the min-Cartesian tree of a. For a subarray [L,R] with minimum at p, the peak lies on one side of p. If it lies left, [p,R] is the descending tail and optimally receives the smallest R-p+1 currently available bottles. Its largest bottle has sorted rank n-(p-L), is placed at p, and the larger bottles recurse on [L,p-1]. Since a_p is the subarray minimum, the discarded tail is feasible exactly when b_t <= a_p, costing one cap exactly on equality. The right case is symmetric. The official alternative fixes the mountain split, replaces a by suffix minima on the increasing side and prefix minima on the decreasing side, and turns each adjusted capacity into a maximum acceptable bottle rank c_i. Perfect assignment is equivalent to |{i:c_i<=v}|<=v for every v. Monotonic stacks maintain capacities as the split moves, while lazy segment trees maintain Hall slacks for b and b-1. Once the all-decremented instance is feasible, every original-height slack is at least -1, and each -1 corresponds bijectively to one sorted equality b_j=d_j and hence one mandatory cap removal.
annotation.complexity: Cartesian-tree variant: O(n log n) time for sorting plus O(n) tree construction/DP, and O(n) memory. Official split-sweep variant: O(n log n) time and O(n) memory from O(n) total monotonic-stack changes and logarithmic range updates.
tricks: sort bottles once because decrementing by one cannot reverse their relative order
represent a mountain by inserting values from largest to smallest at interval endpoints
split at the minimum a-value and discard one side using the smallest remaining bottles
turn strict feasibility into b_t <= a_p, with equality costing exactly one cap
propagate fixed-split ceilings using suffix and prefix minima
encode nested assignment as Hall slacks over ranks
count mandatory removals as original-height slacks equal to -1
uncertainty_points: No unresolved correctness uncertainty remains for the Cartesian-tree solution after the exchange proof and exhaustive/random comparison.
The official analysis specifies the stack/rollback sweep at a high level; this review validates its invariant and rank-counter formula, but not one particular implementation's event encoding.
Official Observation J.1 states thresholds for v in 1..n; a robust implementation must also represent v=0 so a position accepting no bottle is rejected.
review_risks: The node condition is b_t <= a_p, not b_t < a_p: equality is feasible only after removing that bottle's cap.
The discarded side receives the smallest currently available bottles, leaving a suffix of sorted b for recursion; using the global smallest bottles at every node breaks the ranks.
Equal a-values require a deterministic Cartesian-tree convention; only b_i are guaranteed distinct.
A degenerate Cartesian tree can have depth n=5e5, so recursive traversal may overflow the process stack.
In the official sweep, Hall thresholds include v=0, and counting -1 slacks is valid only after all-decremented feasibility is certified.
Suffix-stack operations must be rolled back in exact reverse order.
The official dsu tag is only metadata evidence; neither validated solution uses DSU, so it is intentionally omitted.

solution_variants.name: cartesian-tree-peak-side-dp
solution_variants.summary: Build the min-Cartesian tree of a. At each interval minimum p, choose whether the mountain peak remains in the left or right child; fill the opposite side with the smallest currently available bottles and test it using only its largest bottle at p. Add one exactly when that bottle equals a_p, then take the cheaper feasible child DP.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n log n) time and O(n) memory, with O(n) work after sorting b

solution_variants.name: split-sweep-hall-segment-tree
solution_variants.summary: Sweep every split between the nondecreasing and nonincreasing sides. Maintain induced suffix/prefix-minimum capacities with monotonic stacks and rank-based Hall slacks for original and decremented bottles using lazy suffix range-add/min segment trees; feasible splits expose their cap minimum as the number of original slacks equal to -1.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n log n) time and O(n) memory

tags.evidence: The primary variant decomposes every interval at its minimum a_p; the min-Cartesian tree makes exactly these recurrences explicit and evaluates all nodes in linear total work after sorting.
Each Cartesian-tree node stores the minimum cap cost for its interval and transitions to the feasible left-child or right-child state after fixing the opposite tail.
Once the peak side is chosen at an interval minimum, assigning the smallest available bottles to the discarded tail is a dominance step: any larger replacement cannot improve feasibility or cost.
The official variant updates suffix minima on the growing left side and prefix minima on the shrinking right side; grouped stack merges and rollback reduce these changes from quadratic to O(n) total.
Inserting or deleting a capacity with rank bound c changes every Hall slack C[v] for v>=c. Lazy suffix range-add plus global minimum and minimum-count queries certify feasibility and count -1 slacks.
For a fixed split, positions and bottle ranks form a nested bipartite matching instance; the prefix Hall inequalities are its exact perfect-matching criterion.
Feasibility is characterized by |{i:c_i<=v}|<=v, and the objective follows by proving each original-height slack is either -1 or nonnegative once decremented feasibility holds.
